title: Podman accepts but does not forward ipv6 traffic in rootless mode by default
body:
### Issue Description

When using `curl` or `nc` to send data to a rootless container using `localhost` as the host (e.g. `echo "hello world" | nc localhost 2000`) the tools will fail. It seems like the IPv6 traffic is accepted by podman but never reaches the container. On client side you get a "Connection reset by peer" error. Same happens when explicitly using `::1`.

Slight variations that actually **do** work:
- using `127.0.0.1` instead of `localhost`
- using `0.0.0.0` instead of `localhost`
- `--net=host`
- using rootful mode

OS: Fedora 41
Podman: 5.4.1

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Take any HTTP server and put it in a container, e.g. a simple echo server:
   ```Dockerfile
   FROM ubuntu:24.04

   RUN apt-get update
   RUN apt-get install -y netcat-openbsd findutils
   CMD ["nc", "-l", "2000", "-k"]
   ```
3. `podman build . -t echo`
4. (rootless) `podman run --rm -p 2000:2000 echo`
5. (in another terminal) `echo "hello world" | nc localhost 2000`



### Describe the results you received

```
$ echo "hello world" | nc localhost 2000 
Ncat: Connection reset by peer.
```

(e.g. `echo "hello world" | nc 127.0.0.1 2000 ` succeeds)

### Describe the results you expected

I think this should work by default (if at all possible), the current behaviour is very confusing, especially because it works as expected in rootful mode.

### podman info output

comments.body:
This is because the container is not listening on ipv6 at all and thus refuses all ipv6 connections.

The reason this doesn't work is because connect itself will work for the client as pasta bound the port but then it cannot forward to the container as it does not listen on ipv6 so the connection will be closed again which is why you get `Connection reset by peer`

And root actually doesn't work either if you use `::1` because there is not DNAT support for it at all. The reason why root "works" is because there the connect call would actually fail and thus it tries the next ip for the name, i.e. 127.0.0.1.
The same thing that happens if you run with `--network host`.


To make this more confusing for slirp4netns or custom networks we use the rootlessport forwarded which by default mangles the source ip and remaps all traffic to ipv4 in the container so it works there as well.

But regardless the past a behavior is correct and there isn't really a way to avoid that so close this as this is expected behavior.
For you simply don't use locahost and stick to 127.0.0.1.

To clarify: I'm aware that the service is not listening on ipv6. I'm not expecting the connection over ipv6 to work.

What I meant by "work" is just that the correct ip version for the connection is selected (as is the case with `--net=host`, rootful, or when running the service natively without podman).

Is the issue here that podman just doesn't know if the service is listening on ipv6 and just accepts everything to attempt to forward it?

podman doesn't accept anything, pasta does. And yes it cannot (by design) know if something listens on the container when accepting connections. Pasta is started well before the container and must bind all specified ports because any error (i.e. port is use) must create an error for podman so we do not start the container.

If the container side ever binds or not is not relevant because of this. I am not aware of how to make connect on the host fail for such cases to make it work. 

The only  work around I know of if you don't use ipv6 is to pass `--ipv4-only` to pasta, i.e. `--network pasta:--ipv4-only`. That can also be configured in containers.conf for all containers.

cc @sbrivio-rh

> What I meant by "work" is just that the correct ip version for the connection is selected (as is the case with `--net=host`, rootful, or when running the service natively without podman).

I'm not entirely sure why that would work without Podman or with `--net=host`, by the way, because the selection of the IP address for `localhost` is done by the local resolver in exactly the same way with or without Podman / pasta.

Does the client try to connect to ::1, gets an ICMP port unreachable message, and then tries again with 127.0.0.1, because `localhost` gets resolved to (at least) both addresses?

> If the container side ever binds or not is not relevant because of this. I am not aware of how to make connect on the host fail for such cases to make it work.

I'm not sure if that would make a difference here, but we were considering to implement something like https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=24. There's a problem with that, though: as long as we don't accept() the connection, we don't know what the source address is, so we don't know if the service inside the container / guest would accept that connection.

So, at least for virtual machines (and passt(1)), it looks like we can't do much better than that. But for containers we could actually check bound ports and not accept the connection, perhaps. Assuming that it helps, that is. That's why I'm curious as to why it works with `--net=host` or equivalent.

> Does the client try to connect to ::1, gets an ICMP port unreachable message, and then tries again with 127.0.0.1, because localhost gets resolved to (at least) both addresses?

I am not sure about the ICMP part but as I understand most tools try all ip addresses they resolved for a given name until connect works?!

Looking at strace `nc localhost` in the working case:
```
socket(AF_INET6, SOCK_STREAM, IPPROTO_TCP) = 3
fcntl(3, F_GETFL)                       = 0x2 (flags O_RDWR)
fcntl(3, F_SETFL, O_RDWR|O_NONBLOCK)    = 0
connect(3, {sa_family=AF_INET6, sin6_port=htons(2000), sin6_flowinfo=htonl(0), inet_pton(AF_INET6, "::1", &sin6_addr), sin6_scope_id=0}, 28) = -1 EINPROGRESS (Operation now in progress)
pselect6(4, [3], [3], [3], {tv_sec=10, tv_nsec=0}, NULL) = 2 (in [3], out [3], left {tv_sec=9, tv_nsec=999998540})
getsockopt(3, SOL_SOCKET, SO_ERROR, [ECONNREFUSED], [4]) = 0
close(3)                                = 0
socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, IPPROTO_TCP) = 3
fcntl(3, F_GETFL)                       = 0x2 (flags O_RDWR)
fcntl(3, F_SETFL, O_RDWR|O_NONBLOCK)    = 0
connect(3, {sa_family=AF_INET, sin_port=htons(2000), sin_addr=inet_addr("127.0.0.1")}, 16) = -1 EINPROGRESS (Operation now in progress)
pselect6(4, [3], [3], [3], {tv_sec=10, tv_nsec=0}, NULL) = 1 (out [3], left {tv_sec=9, tv_nsec=999998860})
getsockopt(3, SOL_SOCKET, SO_ERROR, [0], [4]) = 0
```

For `::1` it got ECONNREFUSED then used 127.0.0.1 and tried again.

In the non working case strace seems to affect the nc behavior as it exits with success there despite the remote not getting the message. but it is clear that there that it consider ::1 to be connected because this does not return an error:
`getsockopt(3, SOL_SOCKET, SO_ERROR, [0], [4]) = 0`

> I am not sure about the ICMP part but as I understand most tools try all ip addresses they resolved for a given name until connect works?!

Yes, this is also what `curl` does (can be observed with `-v`). 
It first tries to connect to `::1`, that succeeds (only with rootless podman) so it never goes on to try `127.0.0.1`.

Without rootless podman the connection to `::1` fails.

> > Does the client try to connect to ::1, gets an ICMP port unreachable message, and then tries again with 127.0.0.1, because localhost gets resolved to (at least) both addresses?
> 
> I am not sure about the ICMP part

...or equivalent. If it's a local socket you don't need to have an actual ICMP error on the non-wire to get ECONNREFUSED. With a separate network namespace or a separate node, you would have a "port unreachable" error which the kernel relays to the application as ECONNREFUSED.

> but as I understand most tools try all ip addresses they resolved for a given name until connect works?!

Yes, they would usually call getaddrinfo() and try all the results.

> Looking at strace `nc localhost` in the working case:
> 
> ```
> socket(AF_INET6, SOCK_STREAM, IPPROTO_TCP) = 3
> fcntl(3, F_GETFL)                       = 0x2 (flags O_RDWR)
> fcntl(3, F_SETFL, O_RDWR|O_NONBLOCK)    = 0
> connect(3, {sa_family=AF_INET6, sin6_port=htons(2000), sin6_flowinfo=htonl(0), inet_pton(AF_INET6, "::1", &sin6_addr), sin6_scope_id=0}, 28) = -1 EINPROGRESS (Operation now in progress)
> ```

The "port unreachable" error comes here, and:

> ```
> pselect6(4, [3], [3], [3], {tv_sec=10, tv_nsec=0}, NULL) = 2 (in [3], out [3], left {tv_sec=9, tv_nsec=999998540})
> getsockopt(3, SOL_SOCKET, SO_ERROR, [ECONNREFUSED], [4]) = 0
> ```

the kernel relays that to the application:

> ```
> close(3)                                = 0
> socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, IPPROTO_TCP) = 3
> fcntl(3, F_GETFL)                       = 0x2 (flags O_RDWR)
> fcntl(3, F_SETFL, O_RDWR|O_NONBLOCK)    = 0
> connect(3, {sa_family=AF_INET, sin_port=htons(2000), sin_addr=inet_addr("127.0.0.1")}, 16) = -1 EINPROGRESS (Operation now in progress)
> `...`

...but given that `localhost` also resolved to 127.0.0.1, that one is tried next.

> ```
> pselect6(4, [3], [3], [3], {tv_sec=10, tv_nsec=0}, NULL) = 1 (out [3], left {tv_sec=9, tv_nsec=999998860})
> getsockopt(3, SOL_SOCKET, SO_ERROR, [0], [4]) = 0
> ```

and it succeeds.

> ```
> 
> For `::1` it got ECONNREFUSED then used 127.0.0.1 and tried again.
> 
> In the non working case strace seems to affect the nc behavior as it exits with success there despite the remote not getting the message. but it is clear that there that it consider ::1 to be connected because this does not return an error: `getsockopt(3, SOL_SOCKET, SO_ERROR, [0], [4]) = 0`

Right, yes, we always accept() the connection because if we don't do that we don't know the source address, and not even the destination address we should try to connect to.

This particular case (IPv4 / IPv6 loopback address) is something we could solve in a number of ways (for example, instead of using a dual-stack socket, just in this case, we could just bind to IPv4)... but it's a hack and it won't work in general.

What we would really need is a pair of kernel options for accept(), one could be something like "SO_PEEK" (don't accept the connection, but fill `addr`), and another one similar to "MSG_TRUNC" (discard one connection from the backlog).

The semantic would be obvious, I'm not sure about how complicated the implementation would be and if something like that would ever be accepted. It doesn't look very intrusive from an API perspective though.
